content.text: Azure tags are key-value metadata pairs that you attach to ARM resources, resource groups, subscriptions, and management groups. A tag consists of a name (key) and a value — both are strings. Tags serve as the primary mechanism for organizing Azure resources for cost allocation, operational automation, RBAC scope targeting, and compliance reporting. The Azure Cost Management service can break down spending by tag value, making tagging the foundation of chargeback and showback billing models.

attrs.alt: Azure resource tags anatomy, scope, and inheritance behavior

content.text: Tags are not inherited automatically. A tag on a resource group does not appear on the resources inside it. Each level of the Azure hierarchy (management group, subscription, resource group, resource) maintains its own independent set of tags. This is a frequent source of confusion for new Azure practitioners — explicitly pushing tags down to child resources requires Azure Policy with the Modify effect.
Applies to resources, resource groups, and subscriptions
MGs are outside the standard ARM resource hierarchy
128 chars for storage accounts and App Service resources
Case-insensitive for matching, case-preserved for display
tags['costcenter'] and tags['CostCenter'] are the same key — last write wins
Tags with empty string values are allowed; null removes the tag
Letters, digits, spaces, and most special chars EXCEPT < > % & \ ? /
Use hyphens or underscores as separators in practice

Storage accounts and App Service plans have a 128-character tag key limit rather than 512. When designing a tag schema, keep keys under 128 characters to ensure compatibility across all resource types.
Setting Tags — CLI, Portal, and ARM

Tags can be set at resource creation time or updated on existing resources. The CLI has two approaches: az resource tag (replaces all tags) and az tag update with --operation merge (adds/updates tags without removing existing ones). The merge operation is almost always the correct choice for updating tags on resources that may have other tags you did not set.

# Set tags at resource creation (most CLI create commands accept --tags)
az vm create \
  --name vm-prod-web-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod-web \
  --image Win2022Datacenter \
  --size Standard_D2s_v3 \
  --tags CostCenter=eng-platform Environment=prod Owner=platform-team

# Add/update tags on an existing resource (merge — preserves existing tags)
RESOURCE_ID=$(az vm show --name vm-prod-web-01 --resource-group rg-prod-web --query id -o tsv)

az tag update \
  --resource-id $RESOURCE_ID \
  --operation merge \
  --tags CostCenter=eng-platform Environment=prod

# REPLACE all tags (removes any tags not in this command)
az tag update \
  --resource-id $RESOURCE_ID \
  --operation replace \
  --tags CostCenter=eng-platform
# WARNING: This removes any tags not specified here

# Delete a specific tag from a resource
az tag update \
  --resource-id $RESOURCE_ID \
  --operation delete \
  --tags DeprecatedTagName=''

# Tag a resource group
az group update \
  --name rg-prod-web \
  --set tags.CostCenter='eng-platform' tags.Environment='prod'

# Tag a subscription
az tag update \
  --resource-id /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --operation merge \
  --tags BusinessUnit='engineering' Owner='platform-team'

# Read all tags on a resource
az tag list --resource-id $RESOURCE_ID -o json
Tags in ARM Templates and Bicep

In ARM templates, tags are expressed as a JSON object in the tags property of each resource. In Bicep, tags are assigned using the same property. Parameterizing the tags object allows the same template to be deployed in multiple environments with environment-specific tag values.

Once tags are applied, they become filterable dimensions in the CLI, portal, Resource Graph, and Cost Management. Resource Graph provides the most powerful tag querying — it can return resources across all subscriptions in a management group and join tag data with other resource properties.

# List all resources with a specific tag value
az resource list \
  --tag CostCenter=eng-platform \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Type:type,RG:resourceGroup}' \
  -o table

# List all resources with a specific tag key (any value)
az resource list \
  --tag CostCenter \
  -o table

# Resource Graph: find resources MISSING a required tag
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where isnull(tags['CostCenter']) or tags['CostCenter'] == ''
| where type !in~ ['Microsoft.Resources/deployments', 'Microsoft.Resources/resourceGroups']
| project name, type, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
| order by type asc, name asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table | head -30

# Resource Graph: count resources by tag value (cost center breakdown)
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where isnotempty(tags['CostCenter'])
| summarize ResourceCount = count() by CostCenter = tostring(tags['CostCenter'])
| order by ResourceCount desc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Resource Graph: find resources with inconsistent environment tags
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where isnotempty(tags['Environment'])
| where tags['Environment'] !in~ ['prod', 'production', 'dev', 'development', 'test', 'staging']
| project name, type, tags['Environment'], subscriptionId
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Get all unique tag keys in use across a subscription
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where isnotempty(tags)
| project keys = bag_keys(tags)
| mv-expand key = keys
| summarize Count = count() by TagKey = tostring(key)
| order by Count desc
" --subscriptions $SUB_ID -o table

Tags without enforcement drift over time. Azure Policy provides three mechanisms to enforce tag standards: Deny (block resource creation if required tags are missing), Modify (automatically add or correct tags at create/update time), and AuditIfNotExists is not applicable for tags — use Audit or Modify. The Modify effect is the most user-friendly because it adds missing tags automatically without blocking the resource deployment.

# Assign built-in 'Require a tag on resources' policy (Deny if tag missing)
POLICY_ID=$(az policy definition list \
  --query "[?displayName=='Require a tag on resources'].id" -o tsv | head -1)

az policy assignment create \
  --name require-costcenter-tag \
  --policy $POLICY_ID \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"}}'

# Assign built-in 'Inherit a tag from the resource group' policy (Modify effect)
INHERIT_POLICY=$(az policy definition list \
  --query "[?displayName=='Inherit a tag from the resource group'].id" -o tsv | head -1)

az policy assignment create \
  --name inherit-costcenter-from-rg \
  --policy $INHERIT_POLICY \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"}}' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus2

# Assign the managed identity Contributor role for the Modify effect to work
MI_OID=$(az policy assignment show --name inherit-costcenter-from-rg --management-group mg-corp --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $MI_OID \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp

# Built-in tag policies to know:
# 'Require a tag on resources'                      -> Deny if missing
# 'Require a tag and its value on resources'        -> Deny if value not in allowedValues
# 'Inherit a tag from the resource group'           -> Modify: copy from RG to resource
# 'Inherit a tag from the subscription'             -> Modify: copy from sub to resource
# 'Add or replace a tag on resources'               -> Modify: set tag regardless of current value
# 'Add or replace a tag on resource groups'         -> Modify: set tag on RGs
Relying on tag inheritance (expecting RG tags to appear on resources)
Resources have no tags — Cost Management reports show no cost breakdown
Use Policy with Modify effect to explicitly copy tags from RG to child resources
Some tools and scripts fail to handle keys with spaces; Cost Management may misparse them
Use hyphens or PascalCase: CostCenter, cost-center, or cost_center
Mixing case in tag values ('Prod' vs 'prod' vs 'PROD')
Cost reports split the same environment into multiple categories
Normalize at creation time with a Modify policy that sets toLower() value, or use allowedValues
ARM rejects the resource update or creation with a tag limit error
Design the tag schema to stay under 50; consolidate information into fewer tags if needed
Tagging resource groups but not resources
Cost Management aggregates costs at the resource level — RG tags don't appear on resource-level reports
Tag both RGs and resources; use Policy Modify to keep them in sync
Subscription-level cost reports lose the owner/team dimension
Apply at least Owner and BusinessUnit tags to every subscription

Terraform applies tags via the tags argument, which is a map(string). A common pattern is defining a local for shared tags and merging it with resource-specific overrides using the merge() function. This avoids repeating the same tag set in every resource block and makes it easy to update shared tags in one place.

Azure Cost Management uses tags as dimensions for cost analysis. When you break down costs by tag in the Cost Analysis blade, each unique tag value becomes a group-by category. For this to work correctly, all resources generating costs must have the same tag key and consistent values. Resources without the tag key appear in an 'Untagged' bucket that obscures accountability.

# Export cost data grouped by tag using Azure Cost Management CLI
# (requires az costmanagement extension)
az extension add --name costmanagement 2>/dev/null || true

# Query cost by CostCenter tag for current month
START=$(date -u +%Y-%m-01)
END=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%d)

az costmanagement query \
  --type Usage \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period from=$START to=$END \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost": {"name": "Cost", "function": "Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type": "TagKey", "name": "CostCenter"}]' \
  --query 'properties.rows' -o table

# Find resources with no CostCenter tag — these appear in 'Untagged' in Cost Analysis
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where isnull(tags['CostCenter']) or tags['CostCenter'] == ''
| where type !startswith 'Microsoft.Resources'
| summarize Count = count() by type = tolower(type)
| order by Count desc
" --subscriptions $SUB_ID -o table

# Generate a tag compliance report across all subscriptions in an MG
az graph query -q "
Resources
| extend
    hasCostCenter  = isnotempty(tags['CostCenter']),
    hasEnvironment = isnotempty(tags['Environment']),
    hasOwner       = isnotempty(tags['Owner'])
| summarize
    Total         = count(),
    WithCostCenter  = countif(hasCostCenter),
    WithEnvironment = countif(hasEnvironment),
    WithOwner       = countif(hasOwner),
    FullyTagged   = countif(hasCostCenter and hasEnvironment and hasOwner)
    by sub = subscriptionId
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * FullyTagged / Total, 1)
| order by CompliancePct asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

An effective tag schema balances comprehensiveness with practicality. Too few tags create blind spots in cost allocation and automation. Too many tags (approaching the 50-tag limit) create operational overhead and increase the risk of inconsistency. A standard enterprise schema typically includes five to eight required tags covering the dimensions needed for cost allocation, operational automation, and compliance.
Charge costs to a business unit or project code
Yes — affects automation and policy scope
Identify the team or individual accountable for the resource
Associate resource with a specific application or service
Indicate the provisioning tool for automation
Group VMs for scheduled maintenance windows
Flag temporary resources for cleanup automation
Optional — for sandbox and test resources

When adopting a tag schema for the first time, hundreds or thousands of existing resources may need to be tagged in bulk. The Azure CLI and Resource Graph together enable automated bulk tagging: query untagged resources with Resource Graph, then loop and apply tags. For large-scale operations, PowerShell's parallel foreach or a Logic App workflow is more efficient than a sequential bash loop.

# Bulk tag all resources in a resource group that are missing CostCenter
RESOURCE_GROUP='rg-prod-web'
COST_CENTER='eng-platform'

# Get all resource IDs in the RG (az resource list, not Resource Graph — faster for single RG)
RESOURCE_IDS=$(az resource list \
  --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
  --query "[?tags.CostCenter==null].id" -o tsv)

echo "Found $(echo $RESOURCE_IDS | wc -w | tr -d ' ') resources to tag"

for RID in $RESOURCE_IDS; do
  echo "Tagging: $RID"
  az tag update \
    --resource-id $RID \
    --operation merge \
    --tags CostCenter=$COST_CENTER
done

# Bulk tag via Resource Graph (cross-subscription, MG scope)
# Step 1: Export untagged resource IDs to a file
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where isnull(tags['CostCenter']) or tags['CostCenter'] == ''
| where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources'
| project id
" --management-groups mg-corp --query '[].id' -o tsv > untagged_resources.txt

echo "Total untagged: $(wc -l < untagged_resources.txt)"

# Step 2: Loop and tag (consider batching for 1000+ resources)
while IFS= read -r RESOURCE_ID; do
  az tag update \
    --resource-id "$RESOURCE_ID" \
    --operation merge \
    --tags CostCenter=$COST_CENTER Owner=platform-team
done < untagged_resources.txt

# For very large scale (10k+ resources), use Azure CLI parallel execution:
cat untagged_resources.txt | xargs -P 8 -I{} az tag update \
  --resource-id {} \
  --operation merge \
  --tags CostCenter=$COST_CENTER
# -P 8 runs 8 parallel processes — respect ARM throttling limits
Tag Automation with Azure Automation Runbooks

For ongoing tag maintenance — expiry-based cleanup, auto-tagging new resources, syncing tags with a CMDB — Azure Automation Runbooks provide a serverless scheduled execution environment. A runbook can query Resource Graph for resources needing tag updates and apply them, running on a daily schedule to keep tags in sync with source-of-truth data.

# Azure Automation Runbook: tag new resources based on their resource group tags
# This runbook is designed to run daily and inherit RG tags to any resource
# that is missing the CostCenter tag

param([string]$ManagementGroupId = "mg-corp")

# Connect using system-assigned managed identity
Connect-AzAccount -Identity

# Query for resources missing CostCenter but whose RG has CostCenter
$query = @"
Resources
| where isnull(tags['CostCenter']) or tags['CostCenter'] == ''
| join kind=leftouter (
    ResourceContainers
    | where type =~ 'Microsoft.Resources/resourceGroups'
    | where isnotempty(tags['CostCenter'])
    | project rgId = id, rgCostCenter = tags['CostCenter']
) on \$left.resourceGroup == \$right.rgId
| where isnotempty(rgCostCenter)
| project id, rgCostCenter
"@

$results = Search-AzGraph -Query $query -ManagementGroup $ManagementGroupId

Write-Output "Found $($results.Count) resources to tag"

foreach ($resource in $results) {
    try {
        Update-AzTag -ResourceId $resource.id -Tag @{CostCenter = $resource.rgCostCenter} -Operation Merge
        Write-Output "Tagged: $($resource.id) with CostCenter=$($resource.rgCostCenter)"
    } catch {
        Write-Warning "Failed to tag $($resource.id): $_"
    }
}

Write-Output 'Tag sync runbook complete'

Azure tags are key-value metadata pairs attached independently to management groups, subscriptions, resource groups, and individual resources. Tags are not inherited — a tag on a resource group does not appear on resources inside it. Each resource supports up to 50 tags with keys up to 512 characters (128 for storage and App Service) and values up to 256 characters. Tag key matching is case-insensitive but case-preserved for display; values are case-sensitive. Use az tag update --operation merge to add tags without removing existing ones, and az graph query against the Resources table to find resources missing required tags. Enforce tag standards with Azure Policy: Deny for required-tag enforcement, Modify for automatic tag inheritance, and built-in policies like 'Inherit a tag from the resource group' for common patterns.
